front: Poem: 'The tangled bine-stems scored the sky / Like strings of broken lyres'
Poem: 'The land's sharp features seemed to be the Century's corpse outleant'
Poem: thrush described as 'frail, gaunt, and small,' singing a 'full-hearted evensong / of joy illimited'
Poem: 'The ancient pulse of germ and birth / Was shrunken hard and dry'
Poem: originally published as 'By the Century's Deathbed' on December 19, 1900
Poem: 'darkling' — same unusual adjective used for a plain in 'Dover Beach'
Poem: 'consummation comes, and jars two hemispheres' — ship meets iceberg
back: The Convergence of the Twain (Thomas Hardy)

front: Poem: 'The Immanent Will that stirs and urges everything' drives convergence of ship and iceberg
back: The Convergence of the Twain (Thomas Hardy)

front: Poem: 'The sea-worm crawls' on underwater wreck; written in eleven tercets
back: The Convergence of the Twain (Thomas Hardy)

front: Poem: Simon Armitage wrote a response poem with the same title about 9/11, picturing a 'half-excoriated Apple Mac'
back: The Convergence of the Twain (Thomas Hardy)

front: Poem: corpses hear 'great guns' that 'shook all our coffins as we lay'
Poem: God says 'It will be warmer when I blow the trumpet'
Poem: ends with images of Stourton Tower, Camelot, and 'starlit Stonehenge'
Poem: dead woman wonders who's digging — enemy? loved one? — turns out her dog is burying a bone
back: Ah, Are You Digging on My Grave (Thomas Hardy)
work: Ah, Are You Digging on My Grave

front: Poem: ends with 'Your face, and the God-curst sun, and a tree / And a pond edged with grayish leaves'
Poem: 'a grin of bitterness swept thereby / Like an ominous bird a-wing'
Poem: 'Crass Casualty obstructs the sun and rain / And dicing Time for gladness casts a moan'
Poem: speaker wishes 'some vengeful god would call to me'
Poem: invented the noun 'Powerfuller'
Poem: imagines figure warning 'Know that thy sorrow is my ecstasy'
Poem: war is 'quaint and curious' — 'You shoot a fellow down / You'd treat if met where any bar is'; inspired by Boer War
Novel: Little Father Time murders two children and hangs himself
Novel: note reads 'Done because we are too menny'
Novel: set partly in 'Christminster' (thinly disguised Oxford)
Novel: title character is a stonemason aspiring to university
Novel: Sue Bridehead reads Swinburne's 'Thou has conquered, O pale Galilean'
Novel: turns to religion after children murdered
Novel: Arabella returns from Australia with somber child 'Little Father Time'
Novel: wife leaves protagonist's corpse for Remembrance Week party
Novel: Gabriel Oak — rejected shepherd, third suitor alongside Boldwood and Sergeant Troy
back: Far from the Madding Crowd (Thomas Hardy)

front: Novel: Bathsheba sends Boldwood a Valentine saying 'Marry Me'
back: Far from the Madding Crowd (Thomas Hardy)

front: Novel: Fanny Robin's coffin found to contain her child — Gabriel tries to cover it up
back: Far from the Madding Crowd (Thomas Hardy)

front: Novel: Clym Yeobright returns to Egdon Heath from Paris (hated selling diamonds)
back: The Return of the Native (Thomas Hardy)

front: Novel: marries Eustacia Vye who drowns with Damon Wildeve
back: The Return of the Native (Thomas Hardy)

front: Novel: opens and ends on Guy Fawkes Day
back: The Return of the Native (Thomas Hardy)

front: Novel: includes sheep-dye salesman perpetually stained red
back: The Return of the Native (Thomas Hardy)

front: Poem: elegiac sequence for dead wife Emma Gifford
Poem: epigraph uses Dido's 'veteris vestigia flammae'
Poem: 'hereto I come to view a voiceless ghost' — speaker retraces romantic trip along cliffs of Cornwall
Author: 'The Ruined Maid' — ends 'a raw country girl, such as you be, / Cannot quite expect that. You ain't ruined'
Author: 'The Blinded Bird' protests blinding of finches to improve singing (vinkensport)
Author: 'Who hopeth, endureth all things?'
Author: 'In Tenebris' — 'death will not appal one who, past doubtings all, waits in unhope'
Author: 'The Pity of It' (1915) — uses 'Thu bist' and 'Er war' to show shared English-German heritage
Author: A Pair of Blue Eyes — origin of term 'cliffhanger'
Author: Henry Knight rescued by Elfride's underwear
Author: 'A Mere Interlude' — Baptista elopes with former lover who dies an hour after wedding
Author: also an architect — designed Max Gate
Author: called Charlotte Mew 'far and away the best living woman poet'
